best_bets.why_this: It combines the largest team-quality gap on the slate with plus-money pricing. The market is charging a premium on the Dodgers moneyline, but the run line still appears materially undervalued given the mismatch and the Angels' unresolved starting-pitcher situation.

Bryan Woo's 2.96 FIP / 2.91 xERA is the single best pitching indicator on the entire slate, and he faces a below-average Framber Valdez (4.18 FIP) with a rested bullpen behind him. The 20-cent gap between my fair line (-155) and market (-128) is the widest edge I found, and it's backed by a 30-run differential gap between the teams.

The market is pricing Arizona as a moderate favorite despite Kelly’s xERA and contact profile both signaling significant downside, making the dog price look meaningfully off even after accounting for home-field and variance.

Woo's superior xERA, barrel suppression, and SEA's 8-2 last-10 form create the slate's largest probability gap versus market price, with fresh bullpens on both sides removing the risk factor present in every other candidate.
The Yankees lead every meaningful team metric by a wide margin, Gray is the more trustworthy starter, and the line is softer than it should be for a home favorite of this quality.

Singer's badness is confirmed across ERA, FIP, xERA and recent form rather than resting on one noisy metric, making it the lowest-variance pricing miss on the board — cleaner than the Seattle lean (market already favors them) and the Washington dog (relies more on contact-luck regression).

A note on the shape of this card, outside the format: nothing on this slate cleared the 5+ point gap with confirmed data needed for a 3- or 5-unit play, so it's three disciplined 1-unit plays, one lean, and a lot of passes. I also skipped the optional parlay deliberately — multiplying two thin ~1-unit edges compounds the vig and erases the value, so the two best singles stand alone.

Anthony Kay's extreme underlying metrics (5.23 FIP, 5.67 xERA) scream regression against a Jesús Luzardo (2.77 FIP, 3.16 xERA) who is legitimately pitching at an ace level, creating a massive mismatch that the market hasn't fully priced in on the run line.

Bryan Woo's ERA, FIP, and xERA all agree over 70 innings that he is a legitimately elite starter right now, and all three metrics cluster tightly between 2.91 and 3.44 — not a case where one number flatters while others warn. Framber Valdez is consistently mediocre across the same three measures with a 4.58 ERA over his last three starts, Seattle is 8-2 in their last 10, and Detroit's offense (3.9 RS/G, -25 run diff) gives Woo a favourable matchup with no tier-reduction trigger. The -128 best price represents the clearest gap between market price and estimated fair value on this slate.
